title: Head Injury — Severe
oneLiner: Major traumatic brain injury (GCS ≤8) requiring immediate neuroprotection, airway control, and urgent neurosurgical input.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Raised intracranial pressure (ICP) causing transtentorial herniation.**  
  *Single finding:* one fixed, dilated pupil (ipsilateral to mass) or bilateral fixed pupils.  
- **Airway obstruction / hypoxia / hypotension (secondary brain injury).**  
  *Single finding:* SpO₂ < 90% or systolic BP < 90 mmHg despite initial resuscitation.  
  **Both must be ruled out before moving to routine assessment.**
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Loss of consciousness (often prolonged, >30 min)
  - Seizures (any type)
  - Persistent vomiting
  - Severe headache (in those able to report)
  - Amnesia (retrograde > anterograde)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **GCS ≤ 8** (or drop by ≥ 2 points) — coma state
  - **Pupillary asymmetry** (anisocoria >1mm) or sluggish/fixed unreactive pupil
  - Focal motor deficits (hemiparesis, decerebrate/decorticate posturing)
  - Signs of basilar skull fracture: periorbital ecchymosis ("raccoon eyes"), retro-auricular bruising (Battle’s sign), CSF rhinorrhoea/otorrhoea
  - Cushing’s triad (late sign): bradycardia, hypertension, irregular respiration
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Traumatic brain injury (TBI):** subdural haematoma (SDH), epidural haematoma (EDH), cerebral contusion, diffuse axonal injury (DAI) — all require CT
  - **Non-traumatic causes of coma:** spontaneous intracerebral haemorrhage (e.g., hypertensive, amyloid), ischaemic stroke with malignant oedema, meningitis/encephalitis, hypoglycaemia, drug overdose
- **Splitter:** “Clear history of high-energy trauma + GCS ≤8? Nearly always TBI. No trauma + sudden coma? Think stroke, hypoglycaemia, or seizure first.”
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **ABG** — PaO₂ > 11 kPa, PaCO₂ 4.5–5.0 kPa, lactate
  - **Blood glucose** (fingerprick) — exclude hypoglycaemia
  - **Pupillary and GCS chart** every 15–30 min initially
  - **Spinal immobilisation** until cervical spine cleared (CT c-spine if obtunded)
- **Bloods:**
  - **FBC, coagulation screen, U&Es, LFTs, glucose, ABG** — mandatory
  - **Group & save** (crossmatch if active bleeding)
  - **Toxicology screen** if no history
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT head (within 1 hour of arrival — NICE NG39) with cervical spine CT
  - **Second-line:** CT angiogram if suspected traumatic arterial injury (e.g., carotid/vertebral)
  - **MRI** — not in acute phase; used for diffuse axonal injury after stabilisation
- **Special tests:**
  - **ICP monitoring** — inserted by neurosurgery if GCS ≤8 with abnormal CT
- **Never-miss test:** **CT head** in any patient with GCS <15 after head trauma — omission risks missing a surgical mass lesion.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABCDE approach** — secure airway (ET intubation if GCS ≤8 or unable to protect airway), maintain oxygenation (SpO₂ > 94%), avoid hypotension
  - **Spinal immobilisation** until full clearance (clinical + CT)
  - **Control seizures** (lorazepam 2–4 mg IV, or levetiracetam if established status)
  - **Transfer to neurosurgical centre** if: GCS ≤8, open/depressed skull fracture, suspected mass lesion, CSF leak, or focal neurology
  - **Mannitol** 0.5–1 g/kg IV bolus *only* for signs of herniation (fixed pupil, Cushing’s) — [check local policy for dose and administration] (do not use in hypotensive patients without careful monitoring)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **ICU/HDU admission** for all severe head injury
  - **Targets:** SBP > 100 mmHg, MAP > 80 mmHg, PaO₂ > 11 kPa, PaCO₂ 4.5–5.0 kPa
  - **ICP monitoring** (if indicated) — keep ICP < 20–22 mmHg
  - **Avoid secondary insults:** hyperglycaemia, fever, hypoxaemia, hypotension
  - **Seizure prophylaxis** — NOT routine (NICE recommends only if seizures occur); use levetiracetam or phenytoin only if indicated
  - **VTE prophylaxis** — mechanical (TED stockings, intermittent pneumatic compression) + chemical (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) after 24–48h if no active bleeding
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **MDT rehabilitation** — physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech and language therapy
  - **Neuropsychology** for cognitive deficits
  - **Driving ban** (DVLA regulations) — patient advised not to drive until assessed; may need re-testing
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% NaCl IV at 1 L/8h (adjust to normovolaemia) — avoid dextrose-containing solutions
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** as above
  - **Diet:** NBM until formal swallow assessment by SALT (risk of aspiration with reduced consciousness)
  - **Observations:** GCS + pupil check every 15 min for 2h, then every 30 min for 2h, then hourly for 4h, then 2-hourly (NICE: if GCS ≤8, continuous monitoring)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Mannitol (for raised ICP/herniation only):** 0.5–1 g/kg IV over 15–30 min — [check local policy for dose and frequency, usually 100–200 mL of 20% solution]
  - **Hypertonic saline (3% NaCl):** alternative or adjunct — [check local protocol] (typically 150–250 mL IV bolus for acute ICP crisis; use cautiously due to hypernatremia risk)
  - **Levetiracetam (seizure):** loading dose 20 mg/kg IV, then maintenance 500 mg–1.5 g IV BD
  - **Lorazepam (acute seizure):** 2–4 mg IV (may repeat once)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Phenytoin (15–20 mg/kg IV loading) — but slower onset, more drug interactions
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Mannitol:** avoid in anuria, pulmonary oedema, hyponatraemia; can cause hyperosmolality and acute kidney injury
  - **Phenytoin:** CYP3A4 inducer; watch for interactions with warfarin, antiepileptics; liver impairment adjust dose
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Mannitol** — contraindicated in anuric renal failure; use with caution in CKD (may accumulate)
  - **Levetiracetam** — dose reduce if CrCl < 50 mL/min (e.g., 50% dose if CrCl 30–49)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Overcorrection of hyponatraemia with hypertonic saline** — rapid correction can cause osmotic demyelination; correct at ≤ 8–10 mmol/L in 24h and stop Bolus if Na > 145
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Herniation** (uncal, central)
  - **Seizures** (early post-traumatic)
  - **Infection** (meningitis from CSF leak; wound infection after surgery)
  - **Coagulopathy** (especially in polytrauma with concurrent anticoagulation)
  - **Airway obstruction, aspiration pneumonia**
  - **Cervical spine injury** (must be cleared)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Post-traumatic epilepsy** (up to 10% risk with severe TBI)
  - **Chronic subdural haematoma** (especially in elderly or those on anticoagulants)
  - **Cognitive deficits** (memory, executive function, processing speed)
  - **Mood disorders** (depression, aggression, apathy)
sections.Prognosis: - **Mortality:** 30–50% for GCS 3–8 (worse with older age, absent pupillary light reflex, hypotension); survivors often have significant disability — about 20% achieve good recovery at 6 months.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **ALWAYS do a rapid pupillary and GCS chart** before sedating or paralyzing — these are your baseline.
- **Check coagulation (INR, APTT, platelets)** before any line insertion or lumbar puncture.
- **Repeat CT head** within 6–24 hours if initial scan is abnormal, or if GCS drops — a delayed haematoma can appear.
- **Consider cervical spine CT** as part of the same scan — movement of an unstable fracture can cause quadriplegia.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give sedation without securing the airway first** — sedation can drop GCS and mask deterioration.
- **Do not allow hypotension (SBP < 100 mmHg)** — even brief hypotension worsens outcomes; aggressive fluid resuscitation is key.
- **Do not hyperventilate (PaCO₂ < 4.0 kPa)** except as a TEMPORARY bridge to surgical decompression in herniation — causes vasoconstriction and ischaemia.
- **Do not discharge a patient with GCS ≤8** — they belong in ICU.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
*Note: severe head injury patients are almost never discharged directly; this section applies to step-down from ED to ward or day-case after stabilisation.*  
- **Return if:** any worsening headache, vomiting, seizure, confusion, or new focal weakness.
- **Follow-up:** neurosurgery clinic within 6 weeks (if not admitted); imaging follow-up if required.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU / neurosurgery immediately if:**
  - **GCS drops by ≥ 2 points** (any time)
  - **New pupillary asymmetry** (single dilated pupil)
  - **Seizure activity** (any type — treat, then escalate)
  - **Systemic instability:** systolic BP < 100 mmHg, SpO₂ < 94% on oxygen, HR > 130 or < 50
  - **Raised ICP** (if monitored) > 22 mmHg for > 5 minutes
- **Trigger for ICU:**
  - GCS ≤ 8 after resuscitation
  - Need for invasive ventilation or ICP monitoring
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 or signs of impending herniation

rawMarkdown:
## Head Injury — Severe

**One-liner:** Major traumatic brain injury (GCS ≤8) requiring immediate neuroprotection, airway control, and urgent neurosurgical input.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Raised intracranial pressure (ICP) causing transtentorial herniation.**  
  *Single finding:* one fixed, dilated pupil (ipsilateral to mass) or bilateral fixed pupils.  
- **Airway obstruction / hypoxia / hypotension (secondary brain injury).**  
  *Single finding:* SpO₂ < 90% or systolic BP < 90 mmHg despite initial resuscitation.  
  **Both must be ruled out before moving to routine assessment.**

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Loss of consciousness (often prolonged, >30 min)
  - Seizures (any type)
  - Persistent vomiting
  - Severe headache (in those able to report)
  - Amnesia (retrograde > anterograde)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **GCS ≤ 8** (or drop by ≥ 2 points) — coma state
  - **Pupillary asymmetry** (anisocoria >1mm) or sluggish/fixed unreactive pupil
  - Focal motor deficits (hemiparesis, decerebrate/decorticate posturing)
  - Signs of basilar skull fracture: periorbital ecchymosis ("raccoon eyes"), retro-auricular bruising (Battle’s sign), CSF rhinorrhoea/otorrhoea
  - Cushing’s triad (late sign): bradycardia, hypertension, irregular respiration

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Traumatic brain injury (TBI):** subdural haematoma (SDH), epidural haematoma (EDH), cerebral contusion, diffuse axonal injury (DAI) — all require CT
  - **Non-traumatic causes of coma:** spontaneous intracerebral haemorrhage (e.g., hypertensive, amyloid), ischaemic stroke with malignant oedema, meningitis/encephalitis, hypoglycaemia, drug overdose
- **Splitter:** “Clear history of high-energy trauma + GCS ≤8? Nearly always TBI. No trauma + sudden coma? Think stroke, hypoglycaemia, or seizure first.”

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **ABG** — PaO₂ > 11 kPa, PaCO₂ 4.5–5.0 kPa, lactate
  - **Blood glucose** (fingerprick) — exclude hypoglycaemia
  - **Pupillary and GCS chart** every 15–30 min initially
  - **Spinal immobilisation** until cervical spine cleared (CT c-spine if obtunded)
- **Bloods:**
  - **FBC, coagulation screen, U&Es, LFTs, glucose, ABG** — mandatory
  - **Group & save** (crossmatch if active bleeding)
  - **Toxicology screen** if no history
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT head (within 1 hour of arrival — NICE NG39) with cervical spine CT
  - **Second-line:** CT angiogram if suspected traumatic arterial injury (e.g., carotid/vertebral)
  - **MRI** — not in acute phase; used for diffuse axonal injury after stabilisation
- **Special tests:**
  - **ICP monitoring** — inserted by neurosurgery if GCS ≤8 with abnormal CT
- **Never-miss test:** **CT head** in any patient with GCS <15 after head trauma — omission risks missing a surgical mass lesion.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABCDE approach** — secure airway (ET intubation if GCS ≤8 or unable to protect airway), maintain oxygenation (SpO₂ > 94%), avoid hypotension
  - **Spinal immobilisation** until full clearance (clinical + CT)
  - **Control seizures** (lorazepam 2–4 mg IV, or levetiracetam if established status)
  - **Transfer to neurosurgical centre** if: GCS ≤8, open/depressed skull fracture, suspected mass lesion, CSF leak, or focal neurology
  - **Mannitol** 0.5–1 g/kg IV bolus *only* for signs of herniation (fixed pupil, Cushing’s) — [check local policy for dose and administration] (do not use in hypotensive patients without careful monitoring)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **ICU/HDU admission** for all severe head injury
  - **Targets:** SBP > 100 mmHg, MAP > 80 mmHg, PaO₂ > 11 kPa, PaCO₂ 4.5–5.0 kPa
  - **ICP monitoring** (if indicated) — keep ICP < 20–22 mmHg
  - **Avoid secondary insults:** hyperglycaemia, fever, hypoxaemia, hypotension
  - **Seizure prophylaxis** — NOT routine (NICE recommends only if seizures occur); use levetiracetam or phenytoin only if indicated
  - **VTE prophylaxis** — mechanical (TED stockings, intermittent pneumatic compression) + chemical (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) after 24–48h if no active bleeding
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **MDT rehabilitation** — physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech and language therapy
  - **Neuropsychology** for cognitive deficits
  - **Driving ban** (DVLA regulations) — patient advised not to drive until assessed; may need re-testing
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% NaCl IV at 1 L/8h (adjust to normovolaemia) — avoid dextrose-containing solutions
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** as above
  - **Diet:** NBM until formal swallow assessment by SALT (risk of aspiration with reduced consciousness)
  - **Observations:** GCS + pupil check every 15 min for 2h, then every 30 min for 2h, then hourly for 4h, then 2-hourly (NICE: if GCS ≤8, continuous monitoring)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Mannitol (for raised ICP/herniation only):** 0.5–1 g/kg IV over 15–30 min — [check local policy for dose and frequency, usually 100–200 mL of 20% solution]
  - **Hypertonic saline (3% NaCl):** alternative or adjunct — [check local protocol] (typically 150–250 mL IV bolus for acute ICP crisis; use cautiously due to hypernatremia risk)
  - **Levetiracetam (seizure):** loading dose 20 mg/kg IV, then maintenance 500 mg–1.5 g IV BD
  - **Lorazepam (acute seizure):** 2–4 mg IV (may repeat once)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Phenytoin (15–20 mg/kg IV loading) — but slower onset, more drug interactions
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Mannitol:** avoid in anuria, pulmonary oedema, hyponatraemia; can cause hyperosmolality and acute kidney injury
  - **Phenytoin:** CYP3A4 inducer; watch for interactions with warfarin, antiepileptics; liver impairment adjust dose
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Mannitol** — contraindicated in anuric renal failure; use with caution in CKD (may accumulate)
  - **Levetiracetam** — dose reduce if CrCl < 50 mL/min (e.g., 50% dose if CrCl 30–49)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Overcorrection of hyponatraemia with hypertonic saline** — rapid correction can cause osmotic demyelination; correct at ≤ 8–10 mmol/L in 24h and stop Bolus if Na > 145

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Herniation** (uncal, central)
  - **Seizures** (early post-traumatic)
  - **Infection** (meningitis from CSF leak; wound infection after surgery)
  - **Coagulopathy** (especially in polytrauma with concurrent anticoagulation)
  - **Airway obstruction, aspiration pneumonia**
  - **Cervical spine injury** (must be cleared)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Post-traumatic epilepsy** (up to 10% risk with severe TBI)
  - **Chronic subdural haematoma** (especially in elderly or those on anticoagulants)
  - **Cognitive deficits** (memory, executive function, processing speed)
  - **Mood disorders** (depression, aggression, apathy)

### Prognosis
- **Mortality:** 30–50% for GCS 3–8 (worse with older age, absent pupillary light reflex, hypotension); survivors often have significant disability — about 20% achieve good recovery at 6 months.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **ALWAYS do a rapid pupillary and GCS chart** before sedating or paralyzing — these are your baseline.
- **Check coagulation (INR, APTT, platelets)** before any line insertion or lumbar puncture.
- **Repeat CT head** within 6–24 hours if initial scan is abnormal, or if GCS drops — a delayed haematoma can appear.
- **Consider cervical spine CT** as part of the same scan — movement of an unstable fracture can cause quadriplegia.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give sedation without securing the airway first** — sedation can drop GCS and mask deterioration.
- **Do not allow hypotension (SBP < 100 mmHg)** — even brief hypotension worsens outcomes; aggressive fluid resuscitation is key.
- **Do not hyperventilate (PaCO₂ < 4.0 kPa)** except as a TEMPORARY bridge to surgical decompression in herniation — causes vasoconstriction and ischaemia.
- **Do not discharge a patient with GCS ≤8** — they belong in ICU.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)  
*Note: severe head injury patients are almost never discharged directly; this section applies to step-down from ED to ward or day-case after stabilisation.*  
- **Return if:** any worsening headache, vomiting, seizure, confusion, or new focal weakness.
- **Follow-up:** neurosurgery clinic within 6 weeks (if not admitted); imaging follow-up if required.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU / neurosurgery immediately if:**
  - **GCS drops by ≥ 2 points** (any time)
  - **New pupillary asymmetry** (single dilated pupil)
  - **Seizure activity** (any type — treat, then escalate)
  - **Systemic instability:** systolic BP < 100 mmHg, SpO₂ < 94% on oxygen, HR > 130 or < 50
  - **Raised ICP** (if monitored) > 22 mmHg for > 5 minutes
- **Trigger for ICU:**
  - GCS ≤ 8 after resuscitation
  - Need for invasive ventilation or ICP monitoring
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 or signs of impending herniation
